name: Passage de Clichy – Brassaï
summary: In 1933, Brassaï released Paris by Night, a series of dark, moody street scenes lit by gas lamps. Ordinary moments turned eerie and surreal, like in Passage de Clichy.

name: Université Paris-Nanterre
summary: Birthplace of May '68. Base of operations for Daniel Cohn-Bendit ('Dany le Rouge') and the student movement that ignited the revolt against the Gaullist state.

name: Café de Flore
summary: Daily HQ of Sartre & Simone de Beauvoir from 1942 onwards; the beating heart of Parisian intellectual and existentialist life in the 1950s–60s.

name: L'Écume des Pages
summary: Late-night bookshop (open until midnight) on the Saint-Germain writers' strip, right next to Flore & Deux Magots — perfect after an evening spent near Sartre or Queneau.

name: Les Deux Magots
summary: Quieter alternative to Café de Flore, surrealist haunt of the 1930s, home of the rival 'Les Deux Magots' literary prize. Simone de Beauvoir worked here regularly.

name: Lycée Henri-IV
summary: One of the two most actively protesting schools in May '68, alongside the ENS. A key target of police attempting to break through student barricades in the Latin Quarter.

name: École Normale Supérieure
summary: The 'citadel of Maoism' during May '68; stronghold of Louis Althusser's students. Also the location of the Cinémathèque's 1955 hall, where Godard and Truffaut came of age.

name: Brasserie Lipp
summary: Hierarchical brasserie with 'heaven/purgatory/hell' seating tiers. Politicians dined here — Mitterrand had table #1. Scene of the 1965 disappearance of Moroccan dissident Ben Barka.

name: Place Louis Aragon
summary: A small square on the Île Saint-Louis overlooking the Seine, named in honor of Louis Aragon. It evokes the Parisian wanderings celebrated in his novel Paysan de Paris, and is one of the rare public spaces in Paris dedicated to a surrealist poet.

name: Prison de la Santé
summary: The prison from whose rooftop Michel Vaujour was lifted by helicopter on May 26, 1986, in one of France's most spectacular prison breaks — orchestrated by his wife Nadine, who secretly trained as a helicopter pilot to free him. Still operating today as the last prison inside Paris.

name: Folies Bergère
summary: Paris's first variety theatre, opened in 1869 and thriving by the 1920s — still operating today. Famous for spectacular shows with feathered dancers accessible only to wealthy audiences. Performers over the years included Mata Hari, Isadora Duncan, Josephine Baker, and Russian ballerinas Yulia Sedova and Elizaveta Nikolskaya (Lila Nicolska). The 1929 Art Deco facade was designed by artist-architect Maurice Picaud (Pico), with Lila Nicolska as the model for its central composition.

name: Starbucks Capucines — Most Beautiful in Paris
summary: Widely regarded as the most beautiful Starbucks in Paris, housed in a stunning 19th-century hôtel particulier near Opéra Garnier. Features soaring painted ceilings, ornate mouldings, and plush armchairs — a rare blend of Baroque French architecture and coffee shop culture.

name: Lavatory de la Madeleine — Most Beautiful Public Toilet
summary: Paris's most beautiful public restroom — an underground Art Nouveau gem built in 1905 by Porcher, listed as a Historic Monument. Features mahogany doors, stained glass, decorative ceramics and mosaics. Closed in 2011, fully restored and reopened in 2023. Entry €2.

name: Kiosque des Noctambules — Métro Palais-Royal
summary: The most beautiful metro entrance in Paris, designed by artist Jean-Michel Othoniel for the Paris Métro centenary in 2000. Two domes made of colourful Murano glass beads on aluminium columns — one representing day, one night. Located at Place Colette, at the entrance to Palais Royal – Musée du Louvre station.

name: Le Grand Rex
summary: Europe's largest cinema (3,300 seats), opened December 8, 1932 during the Great Depression. Art Deco building by architect Auguste Bluysen, 32m tall with iconic 2.5m REX letters. Interior designed as an open-air Mediterranean theatre with a starry night ceiling. Used as a Nazi 'Soldatenkino' during WWII. Home to one of France's first escalators (1957) and its largest screen at the time (25×12m, premiered Luc Besson's The Big Blue in 1988). Today hosts films, club nights, escape rooms, and backstage tours.

name: Église Saint-Sulpice — Holy Water Stoups
summary: Home to two giant tridacna clam shells used as holy water stoups — a gift from the Venetian Republic to King Francis I. Marble pedestals carved by Jean-Baptiste Pigalle (teacher of Russian sculptor Fedot Shubin) in the 18th century. Referenced in Jules Verne's Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea and Dan Brown's The Da Vinci Code. Also contains a Pigalle sculpture of Madonna with Child.

name: Marché aux fleurs Reine-Elizabeth-II (Île de la Cité)
summary: Paris’ oldest flower market (since 1809). Metal pavilions added in 1873; current forged-iron & glass-roof pavilions built in 1924 by Jean-Camille Formigé. Renamed after Queen Elizabeth II’s visit (2014); live bird sales banned in 2021. Renovation works underway, planned to finish in 2028.

name: Pont de Bercy — bridge perspective
summary: Strong perspective lines; dynamic background.

name: Philharmonie de Paris — Jean Nouvel building
summary: Futuristic silver architecture at Parc de la Villette.

name: Pont de Bir-Hakeim — cinematic bridge
summary: Iconic bridge used in films; strong lines for photos.

name: Petit Palais — golden door & courtyard
summary: Famous golden gate; intimate inner courtyard.

name: Grande Mosquée de Paris — courtyard & garden
summary: Wisteria in spring; beautiful mosaic courtyard year-round.

name: Place Dalida / Rue Girardon — Montmartre view
summary: Viewpoint over Montmartre / Sacré-Cœur area.

name: Square Rapp — Art Nouveau street
summary: Art Nouveau atmosphere + Eiffel Tower angle.

name: Rue de l'Université — Eiffel Tower view
summary: Classic street view framing the Eiffel Tower.

name: Rue des Mauvais-Garçons
summary: «Улица Плохих Парней» — 33 м в Марэ. Существует с XIII века (тогда rue Chartron, известна проститутками). Две версии названия: буйные подмастерья мясников (XV в.) или банды головорезов при пленении Франциска I (1525). Укорочена втрое при прокладке rue de Rivoli (1854). Здесь жила актриса Рашель до славы. Гравюра Мерьона 1865 г.

name: Au Vieux Paris d'Arcole — maison du chanoine (1512)
summary: Один из старейших домов Парижа, построен в 1512 году как резиденция каноника Нотр-Дам. В 1723 году продан епархией и стал баром à vin. Сейчас — ресторан аверонской кухни. Внесён в реестр исторических памятников. Знаменит глицинией 1946 года (7 м высотой) с особым разрешением мэрии. Адрес: 24, rue Chanoinesse, 75004.

name: Rue des Marmousets — цирюльник и пирожник
summary: Бывшее название rue Chanoinesse. Каноники Нотр-Дам давали кров студентам, которые начали пропадать. Выяснилось, что местный цирюльник убивал их, а пирожник по соседству пускал тела на начинку. Разоблачение произошло после того, как собака два дня выла у двери цирюльника. Одна из самых мрачных городских легенд средневекового Парижа.

name: 1 Rue Chapon — Fake House
summary: A fake residential façade in the northern Marais (3rd arrondissement), used to conceal technical infrastructure while maintaining the visual continuity of the streetscape. The building is indistinguishable from its neighbours at a glance — same stonework, same proportions, but the windows are false and nobody lives inside.

name: 145 Rue La Fayette — Fake House
summary: A false façade on Rue La Fayette in the 10th arrondissement, named after the Marquis de Lafayette who fought in both the American and French Revolutions. The fake house is a standard Haussmann-era front concealing ventilation or access infrastructure for the underground network beneath one of Paris’s long commercial boulevards.

name: 31 Rue du Pont Neuf — Fake House
summary: A false façade in the 1st arrondissement near the Pont Neuf, Paris’s oldest bridge (1607). The fake house hides infrastructure a few steps from the Seine, seamlessly integrated into the dense urban fabric of Les Halles and the old trading quarter.

name: 27 Rue Bergère — Fake House
summary: A fake building on Rue Bergère in the 9th arrondissement, near the Foliès Bergère music hall. The street itself is named after the bergeries (sheepfolds) that occupied this land before the 19th-century urbanisation. The false façade hides underground infrastructure behind a seamless Haussmann frontage.

name: 29 Rue Quincampoix — Fake House
summary: A false façade on Rue Quincampoix, one of the most historically charged streets in Paris. In 1719–20, John Law’s Mississippi Company offices were here — the street was so packed with frenzied investors during the financial bubble that people reportedly rented out their hunchbacks as writing desks. The fake house sits in one of the few medieval-era streets that survived Haussmann’s reforms intact.

name: 174 Rue du Faubourg Saint-Denis — Fake House
summary: A false façade on the Faubourg Saint-Denis, one of the oldest and most commercial streets in northern Paris — a road that has led to the Basilica of Saint-Denis since Roman times. The fake house stands on a street of passage d’armes, workshops, and wholesale food businesses, hiding infrastructure behind a conventional residential frontage.

name: 1 Rue des Ursins — Fake House (Île de la Cité)
summary: A false façade on the Île de la Cité, the historic island at the heart of Paris. Rue des Ursins is one of the oldest street names in Paris — it follows the course of a now-buried arm of the Seine. The fake house here conceals infrastructure on one of the island’s quietest and most medieval-feeling lanes, just behind Notre-Dame.

name: 141 Boulevard Diderot — Fake House (Métro Line 1)
summary: A fake residential building on Boulevard Diderot in the 12th arrondissement, concealing a ventilation shaft and access point for Métro Line 1. The façade is Haussmann-style with trompe-l’œil windows; no one lives here and no floors exist behind the stonework. One of several such structures along the Line 1 corridor between Nation and Vincennes.

name: 44 Rue d’Aboukir — Fake House
summary: A false façade in the 2nd arrondissement, named after Napoleon’s Battle of Aboukir (1799). The street runs through the old textile and garment district, now home to wholesale fabric traders. The fake house hides infrastructure behind a period-appropriate frontage indistinguishable from its neighbours.

name: 14 Rue Duvergier — Fake House
summary: A fake building façade in the 19th arrondissement hiding technical infrastructure behind a realistic residential frontage. The 19th is home to several such structures, built during the rapid expansion of Paris’s underground networks in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

name: 54 Rue des Petites Écuries — Fake House
summary: A false façade in the 10th arrondissement concealing infrastructure behind a realistic residential frontage. Rue des Petites Écuries (Street of the Small Stables) is named after the royal stables of Louis XIV that once stood in this neighbourhood.

name: 3 Rue de l’Aqueduc — Fake House
summary: A fake residential façade in the 10th arrondissement, built to mask technical infrastructure behind a convincing Haussmann-style frontage. The street name — Rue de l’Aqueduc — is itself a clue: it follows the route of one of the ancient aqueducts that once supplied Paris with water from the south.

name: 53 Rue des Archives — Fake House
summary: A false façade on Rue des Archives in the Marais, hiding infrastructure behind a Haussmann-style residential front. Rue des Archives runs through the heart of the historic Jewish quarter of Paris and past the Archives nationales, one of the oldest archive repositories in Europe.

name: 78 Rue la Condamine — Fake House (Métro Line 2)
summary: One of Paris’s most famous fake houses — a Haussmann-era façade with painted windows and no depth behind it, concealing a ventilation and access shaft for Métro Line 2. Built when the underground line was constructed in the early 1900s, it was designed to blend seamlessly into the neighbourhood streetscape. The windows are painted trompe-l’œil, the shutters are fixed. Passers-by rarely notice.

name: La Rotonde — Montparnasse Bohemian Café
summary: One of the four great bohemian cafés of Montparnasse (with Le Dôme, Le Select and La Coupole), La Rotonde was a gathering point for the École de Paris in the 1910s–20s. Modigliani would sit here for hours sketching customers in exchange for drinks. Lenin and Trotsky played chess here before the Revolution. Picasso, Matisse, Kisling, Soutine and Foujita were all regulars. The owner Victor Libion extended credit to broke artists and accepted paintings as payment — which he often simply threw out.

name: 10 Rue de la Grande Chaumière — Académie Chaumiere
summary: The Académie de la Grande Chaumière, a legendary free-model drawing school on the same street as Modigliani’s studio (no. 8). Jeanne Hébuterne studied here, which is how she met Modigliani. Gauguin, Matisse, Giacometti, Cézanne and Rodin all worked or studied on this street. The school remains open today — still running open life-drawing sessions with no registration required. One of the last surviving ateliers of the Montparnasse art world.

name: Musée de la Vie Romantique — George Sand’s Circle
summary: The former home and studio of Dutch painter Ary Scheffer, who lived here from 1830 to 1858. Every Friday evening he hosted the cream of Romantic Paris: George Sand, Chopin, Liszt, Turgenev, Dickens and Delacroix all attended his salons. The museum holds personal objects of George Sand including jewellery, paintings and casts of her hand and Chopin’s hand. The garden café is one of the most pleasant in Paris — free entry to the permanent collection.

name: Le Dôme Café — Heart of the École de Paris
summary: The oldest and most storied of Montparnasse’s bohemian cafés, founded in 1897. Foujita, Modigliani, Soutine, Picasso and Diego Rivera spent evenings here arguing, drinking and sketching. Modigliani drank here heavily in his final years, often trading small drawings for wine. Hemingway wrote about the Montparnasse café scene in The Sun Also Rises. Henry Miller was a regular in the 1930s. Still operating as a brasserie, it retains its Belle Époque interior.

name: Kodawari Ramen Tsukiji
summary: Acclaimed Japanese ramen restaurant near the Palais Royal, themed around the Tsukiji fish market in Tokyo. One of the best ramen spots in Paris, known for its rich tonkotsu broth and immersive interior design replicating a Japanese alleyway. Consistently long queues.

name: Héliport de Paris — First Circular Flight in History
summary: On January 13, 1908, Henri Farman made the first circular airplane flight in history on this ground — a one-kilometre loop lasting one minute, returning to the start point. All previous flights had been in straight lines; Farman proved heavier-than-air machines could be controlled and turned. The feat won him the Deutsch-Archdeacon prize of 50,000 francs. The same terrain later became the Paris Heliport, entirely surrounded by the commune of Issy-les-Moulineaux. A street bordering Paris and Issy is named after Farman. Marthe Richard — prostitute, aviator, spy and politician — trained here after obtaining her pilot’s licence in 1913, becoming one of the first female aviators in France.

name: Fontaine Saint-Sulpice
summary: Built 1843–48 by architect Louis Visconti on Napoleon’s orders, this monumental fountain features four bishops of Louis XIV’s reign facing the cardinal points — none of whom ever became cardinals, hence its Parisian nickname “Les Quatre-Points-Cardinaux” (the four non-cardinals). Three tiers with lion sculptures holding Paris’s coat of arms, water pouring from medieval theatre masks. Dan Brown used the church and fountain as a central location in The Da Vinci Code (2003), bringing international attention to a previously overlooked corner of the 6th arrondissement.

name: 8 Rue Amyot — Jeanne Hébuterne’s Last Home
summary: The family home of Jeanne Hébuterne, Modigliani’s companion and muse. After Modigliani died at 8 Rue de la Grande Chaumière on January 24, 1920, the nine-months-pregnant Jeanne returned here to her parents’ apartment. The following morning she threw herself from the fifth-floor window. She was 21. She and Modigliani are buried together in Père-Lachaise. Her family, who had never accepted the relationship, initially refused to allow her to be buried alongside him; they relented ten years later.

name: Rue du Vieux-Colombier — Hôtel de Tréville & Porthos
summary: Two key Musketeers addresses on the same street. The Hôtel de Tréville — headquarters of the King’s Musketeers where d’Artagnan presents his father’s letter of recommendation — is placed here by Dumas. The novel describes the courtyard as filled with swordsmen crossing blades. Porthos, the most vain and extravagant of the three musketeers, also lives in a “sumptueux appartement” on this street. The historical Tréville’s real address was 31 Rue de Tournon — Dumas moved it here for narrative purposes.

name: Rue Servandoni — D’Artagnan’s Lodging
summary: The real street where Dumas places d’Artagnan’s first Paris lodging — a “petite mansarde” in what the novel calls the Rue des Fossoyeurs (Street of the Gravediggers). The street was renamed Rue Servandoni in 1806 after the Italian architect who redesigned the facade of Saint-Sulpice. It is here that d’Artagnan installs himself on arrival from Gascony, in a garret barely large enough to contain a bed and a trunk, before his fateful first encounters with the three musketeers.

name: Palais Royal — Cardinal Richelieu’s Palace
summary: Originally built as the Palais Cardinal by Richelieu (1633–42), renamed Palais Royal when he bequeathed it to Louis XIII. In The Three Musketeers, it is the nerve centre of the Cardinal’s perfidious scheming against the queen and her allies. Dumas’s Richelieu orchestrates his plots from here, deploying his spy Milady de Winter to compromise Anne d’Autriche. The Palais Royal’s arcaded gardens were later famous as a centre of gambling, prostitution and political pamphlets — the Café de Foy here was where Camille Desmoulins gave the speech that launched the storming of the Bastille in 1789.

name: Église Saint-Joseph-des-Carmes — First Duel of the Musketeers
summary: Dumas sets the famous first duel — in which d’Artagnan simultaneously faces Athos, Porthos and Aramis and the three become friends — in front of the Couvent des Carmes-Déchaux, described as “a sort of building without windows, bordered by arid meadows.” Only the church survives today: Église Saint-Joseph-des-Carmes (1613–20), the first church built in Paris after the Edict of Nantes. During the September Massacres of 1792, the garden was the site of the slaughter of 191 priests. Their bones are preserved in a crypt beneath the chapel. The Institut Catholique de Paris now occupies the former convent grounds.

name: Rue Férou — Athos’s Lodging
summary: Dumas places Athos — the most noble and melancholic of the three musketeers, a man with a tragic secret — in “two small, well-furnished rooms in a furnished house” on Rue Férou, just steps from the Luxembourg Gardens. The street is a short, quiet cut between Rue de Vaugirard and Place Saint-Sulpice. In 1971, Man Ray died at no. 6 — and Hemingway had lived at no. 6 in the early 1920s, in a one-room flat above a sawmill, writing the stories that became In Our Time.

name: 6 Place des Vosges — Milady de Winter’s Residence
summary: Dumas places Milady de Winter — the Cardinal’s beautiful, ruthless and branded spy, arguably the novel’s most compelling character — at no. 6 of what he calls la place Royale, “then the most fashionable quarter.” The real no. 6 was the house of Victor Hugo from 1832 to 1848, now the Maison de Victor Hugo museum. The Place des Vosges, completed in 1612 under Henri IV, was indeed the most prestigious address in Paris in the 1620s — Dumas’s choice was historically precise.

name: Place du Général Catroux — The Three Dumas
summary: This square in the 17th arrondissement once displayed three Dumas monuments and was informally called “Place des Trois Dumas”. At its centre stood a monument to General Alexandre Dumas (the grandfather) — a Napoleonic general and the son of a Haitian slave — which was later removed. The two flanking monuments to Alexandre Dumas-père (author of The Three Musketeers and The Count of Monte Cristo) and Alexandre Dumas-fils (author of La Dame aux Camélias) remain. Three writers from the same family, three monuments, three generations.

name: 70 Rue de Vaugirard — Couvent des Carmes Déchaux
summary: Site of the Convent of the Discalced Carmelites (“Carmes Déchaux” or “Déchaussés” — barefoot friars), referenced by Dumas in The Three Musketeers as a location connected to the religious and clandestine world of 17th-century Paris. During the September Massacres of 1792, over 190 priests and clergy were slaughtered in the convent gardens — their bones are preserved in a crypt beneath the chapel. The Institut Catholique de Paris now occupies the site.

name: 1 Quai Voltaire — D’Artagnan’s House Plaque
summary: A commemorative plaque on the corner building of Rue du Bac and Quai Voltaire marks the site where the real d’Artagnan — Charles de Batz-Castelmore d’Artagnan, captain-lieutenant of Louis XIV’s Musketeers — lived until his death at Maastricht in 1673. The location is strategically apt: directly by the Pont Royal across the Seine, facing the Louvre — the heart of his royal service. Immortalised by Alexandre Dumas in The Three Musketeers (1844).

name: 15 Rue du Bac — Musketeers’ Barracks
summary: Nos. 13–17 Rue du Bac housed the barracks of the King’s Musketeers, where most of them were quartered at royal expense. This arrangement was formalised during d’Artagnan’s tenure as captain-lieutenant of the Musketeers from 1667, and barracks on this site were in use from around 1670. The historical Charles de Batz-Castelmore d’Artagnan was appointed captain-lieutenant by Louis XIV and was killed besieging Maastricht in 1673. Dumas used the real street in his novel.

name: 30 Rue Montmartre — Porte Montmartre
summary: Site of the Porte Montmartre, a secondary gate of Philippe Auguste’s Right Bank wall on the northern perimeter, positioned on the road leading north toward the village of Montmartre. The gate was one of the smaller postern gates, used primarily for commercial traffic from the northern fields into the city markets. Demolished in the 17th century.

name: 200 Rue Saint-Martin — Porte Saint-Martin (Medieval)
summary: Site of the medieval Porte Saint-Martin of Philippe Auguste’s Right Bank wall — distinct from Louis XIV’s triumphal Porte Saint-Martin arch (1674) 400m to the north. This was a principal gate on the road to Saint-Martin-des-Champs and the northern suburbs. The surrounding Marais streets still follow the curve of the old wall. Demolished when Charles V extended the city walls northward in the 1360s.

name: 148 Rue Saint-Honoré — Porte Saint-Honoré
summary: Site of the Porte Saint-Honoré, the principal western gate of Philippe Auguste’s Right Bank wall, positioned on the road to Saint-Cloud and Versailles. Joan of Arc was wounded by an arrow while attempting to storm this gate on September 8, 1429, trying to retake Paris from the English and Burgundians. The gate was demolished in 1636. A plaque marks the spot where she fell.

name: Place de l’Institut — Left Bank Wall Remnant
summary: Area near the Seine where the Left Bank section of Philippe Auguste’s wall reached the river. The wall descended from the Latin Quarter to the riverside at roughly this point, terminating at a river tower. The Institut de France now occupies the site of the College des Quatre-Nations (1688), which was itself built over the old wall line. Wall fragments have been found in excavations around the Pont des Arts area.

name: Cour du Commerce Saint-André — Tower of Philippe Auguste's Wall
summary: One of the most atmospheric covered passages in Paris, built in 1776 — and hiding one of its greatest secrets: at no. 9, a full circular tower of Philippe Auguste’s 1190 wall protrudes through the building, visible inside a café. This was a tower of the Porte de Buci, the main western gate of the Left Bank wall. Marat had his print shop here; Dr. Guillotin tested his decapitation device on sheep in the passage before it was used on humans.

name: 2 Rue des Écoles — Left Bank Wall Remnant
summary: Location on the Left Bank where the line of Philippe Auguste’s wall crossed the street grid. Rue des Écoles itself follows a roughly east-west axis that corresponds to the southern perimeter of the medieval wall’s left bank stretch. Excavations in this area during 19th and 20th century construction works have repeatedly uncovered wall foundations and tower bases.

name: 135 Rue Saint-Denis — Porte Saint-Denis (Medieval)
summary: Site of the original medieval Porte Saint-Denis of Philippe Auguste’s Right Bank wall — not to be confused with the triumphal arch of the same name built by Louis XIV in 1672, which stands 300m to the north. This was the main northern gate of the city on the road to Saint-Denis and the royal abbey. Charles V later extended the wall northward, making this gate an interior crossing. Demolished in the 16th century.

name: 47 Rue Descartes — Porte Bordelles / Left Bank Gate
summary: Site on the Left Bank corresponding to a postern gate of Philippe Auguste’s wall, near the old Rue de la Montagne-Sainte-Geneviève axis. Paul Verlaine died in a hotel at no. 39 in 1896; Ernest Hemingway lived nearby on Rue du Cardinal Lemoine. The street preserves the ancient topography of the Sainte-Geneviève hill, where the wall ran along the ridge before descending back toward the Seine.

name: 101 Rue Saint-Antoine — Porte Saint-Antoine
summary: Site of the Porte Saint-Antoine, the easternmost gate of Philippe Auguste’s Right Bank wall, positioned on the road to Vincennes. The Bastille fortress was built just outside this gate in 1357–83, originally to defend it. The gate was demolished when the Bastille replaced it as the city’s eastern defence. The entire area around the modern Place de la Bastille sits at the junction of the medieval wall and the road this gate controlled.

name: 172 Rue Saint-Jacques — Porte Saint-Jacques
summary: Site of the Porte Saint-Jacques, the main southern gate of Philippe Auguste’s Left Bank wall (built 1190–1215). It stood at the intersection of the Roman road (now Rue Saint-Jacques) with the wall. Pilgrims departing for Santiago de Compostela passed through this gate. The gate was demolished in 1684. A wall section and fragments were discovered during 19th-century construction works.

name: 36 Rue Jean de la Fontaine — Jean Cocteau
summary: Jean Cocteau — poet, filmmaker, playwright, and one of the most prominent cocaine users in 1920s Paris — lived here. He documented his addiction in the 1930 book Opium: Journal of a Cure, written during his detox at a Saint-Cloud clinic. Cocteau’s circle included Picasso, Coco Chanel, Erik Satie, and Marcel Proust. He directed Blood of a Poet (1930) and Beauty and the Beast (1946) at the height of his powers. He died in 1963, hours after learning of Edith Piaf’s death.

name: 4 Rue Garancière — Talleyrand Birthplace
summary: Charles-Maurice de Talleyrand-Périgord was born here on February 2, 1754. He survived and served every French regime from the Ancien Régime to the July Monarchy — royal minister, bishop, revolutionary, Napoleon’s foreign minister, and finally architect of the post-Napoleonic European order at the Congress of Vienna. His birth family abandoned him to a wet nurse after he damaged his foot as an infant. He was the greatest diplomatic survivor in European history. Bismarck called him the only man who ever truly outmanoeuvred Napoleon.

name: 90 Bd de Port-Royal — Lydia Delectorskaya & Matisse
summary: Lydia Delectorskaya — a Russian émigrée from Tomsk — became Henri Matisse’s model, muse, secretary, nurse and companion from 1935 until his death in 1954. She posed for his monumental mural The Dance. His wife Amélie, who had dismissed her as no threat, eventually left when Lydia became indispensable. When Matisse was diagnosed with cancer in 1941, unable to paint standing, Lydia organised the paper cut-out technique of his final decade — his most celebrated work. He gave her paintings twice a year, knowing she’d inherit nothing. When he died, she was not invited to the funeral. She donated her entire collection — over 100 works — to the Hermitage and the Pushkin Museum. The Soviet government refused her the citizenship she requested in return. She spent the rest of her life in a small Paris flat, refused all interviews, and took her own life in 1998 aged 87.

name: 31 Rue de Tournon — Captain de Tréville (Real)
summary: The real home of Jean-Armand du Peyrer, Comte de Tréville — the historical captain of the Musketeers who inspired Dumas’s character. Dumas incorrectly placed his house on Rue de la Vieille Colombier for dramatic purposes. The actual Tréville commanded the Musketeers under Louis XIII and was a close friend of the king. He was the man d’Artagnan truly sought out upon arriving in Paris.

name: 8 Rue de la Grande Chaumière — Modigliani
summary: Amedeo Modigliani lived and worked at this address during his final years, producing the elongated portraits and nudes that now sell for hundreds of millions. He died here in January 1920 aged 35, of tubercular meningitis, having barely sold a painting in his lifetime. The next morning, his pregnant companion Jeanne Hébuterne threw herself from a fifth-floor window two streets away. The street in Montparnasse was already lined with artists’ ateliers — Gauguin had worked nearby, as had Matisse.

name: 5 bis Rue de Verneuil — Serge Gainsbourg
summary: Serge Gainsbourg lived in this hôtel particulier from 1969 until his death in 1991. He shared it for years with Jane Birkin, who left in 1980. The exterior is covered in graffiti and dedications left by fans since his death — the City of Paris has repeatedly threatened to clean the wall, and fans have repeatedly repainted it. Inside, the house was left exactly as he left it: ashtrays full, whisky bottles on the table, art and books everywhere. His daughter Charlotte eventually opened it as a museum in 2023.

name: 72 Rue de Belleville — Edith Piaf’s Birthplace
summary: According to legend — and a plaque on the building — Édith Piaf was born on the pavement in front of this house on December 19, 1915, delivered by a police officer while her father was away. Official records place her birth at Hôpital Tenon, but the neighbourhood story persists. The plaque reads: “On this spot, on 19 December 1915, in utter poverty, was born Édith Piaf, who would go on to conquer the world.” She grew up in Belleville among street performers and prostitutes before her voice was discovered at the age of 20 on Rue Troyon.

name: 25 Rue de Vaugirard — Aramis (Les Trois Mousquetaires)
summary: In Alexandre Dumas’s The Three Musketeers (1844), this is the address of Aramis — the most refined and devout of the four musketeers, who disguises his clerical ambitions under a soldier’s cape. Dumas was meticulous with Paris geography and used real streets. Rue de Vaugirard is the longest street in Paris (4.36km), running through the 6th and 15th arrondissements past the Luxembourg Gardens.

name: La Ruche — Foujita & the School of Paris
summary: La Ruche (“The Beehive”) — a circular building with 140 small studios, constructed from cast-off materials of the 1900 World’s Fair by sculptor Alfred Boucher — became the cradle of the École de Paris. Tsuguharu Foujita (Léonard Fujita) lived and painted here after arriving from Tokyo in 1913, befriending Chagall, Léger, Soutine, Archipenko and Modigliani. They were mostly penniless, paying rent in paintings. Foujita became famous for his self-portrait style: round glasses, bowl haircut, and cats. He was baptised Catholic in 1959 and designed the Reims Chapel of Notre-Dame-de-la-Paix.

name: 65 Rue Pascal — “Docteur Jekyll et Monsieur Bill” (1957)
summary: Address associated with a case nicknamed “Docteur Jekyll et Monsieur Bill” (1957) — suggesting a criminal or incident of dual identity: a respectable public figure concealing a hidden criminal life. The 13th arrondissement location near the Salpêtrière points toward the medical world. Full case details to be researched.

name: Avenue de l'Observatoire — L'Affaire de l'Observatoire
summary: On October 16, 1959, François Mitterrand claimed his car was ambushed near here and he narrowly escaped by diving into the Luxembourg Gardens. The “assassination attempt” made headlines. It later emerged the attack was entirely staged — organised with Mitterrand’s knowledge by far-right activist Robert Pesquet as a political stunt to raise his profile. The scandal nearly ended his career. He was charged with staging a false attack but was never convicted. The affair was largely forgotten by the time he became president in 1981.

name: 12 Boulevard Saint-Germain — Crime Location
summary: Crime location at the eastern end of Boulevard Saint-Germain near Pont de la Tournelle. Specific case details to be researched.

name: 10 Rue de l'Éperon — Crime Location
summary: Crime location in the 6th arrondissement near Carrefour de l'Odéon. Specific case details to be researched.

name: 28 Rue du Cardinal Lemoine — Crime Location
summary: Crime location in the 5th arrondissement near the Seine. Rue du Cardinal Lemoine runs from the river up toward Place de la Contrescarpe, one of the oldest streets in the Latin Quarter. Specific case details to be researched.

name: 172 Boulevard Saint-Germain — Crime Location
summary: Crime location at or near Café de Flore in the 6th arrondissement. Specific case details to be researched.

name: 25 Rue Jenner — Crime Location
summary: Crime location in the 13th arrondissement near the Salpêtrière hospital complex. Specific case details to be researched.

name: 4 Rue Rollin — Crime Location
summary: Crime location in the 5th arrondissement near Place de la Contrescarpe. Rue Rollin is a short, steep street in the old Latin Quarter. Specific case details to be researched.

name: 151 Boulevard Saint-Germain — Kidnapping of Mehdi Ben Barka
summary: On October 29, 1965, Mehdi Ben Barka — exiled leader of the Moroccan opposition and co-founder of the Tricontinental — was lured to the entrance of Brasserie Lipp by a French journalist. Two French plainclothes police officers then bundled him into a car with Moroccan secret agents. He was never seen again; his body was never found. The affair became de Gaulle’s worst political scandal and implicated the French interior ministry, Moroccan intelligence, and underworld figures. The case remains officially unsolved.

name: Hôtel Salomon de Rothschild — Assassination of President Doumer
summary: On May 6, 1932, President Paul Doumer attended a book fair here honouring WWI veteran writers. Russian émigré Pavel Gorguloff — a mentally unstable nationalist who blamed France for not fighting Bolshevism — fired three shots, fatally wounding the president. Doumer died the next day, the only French president assassinated in the 20th century. Gorguloff was guillotined on September 14, 1932. The affair deepened French anxieties about political violence from émigré communities.

name: Place de la Nation — Guillotine Site of the Terror
summary: Between June 14 and July 27, 1794, this square — then called Place du Trône Renversé (Overturned Throne Square) — hosted the guillotine during the final and bloodiest weeks of the Reign of Terror. 1,306 people were executed here in just 43 days, far more than at Place de la Révolution (now Concorde). The victims included nuns, aristocrats, and ordinary citizens. The massacre ended only with the fall of Robespierre on 9 Thermidor (July 27, 1794). The bodies are buried in a mass grave in the nearby Picpus Cemetery.

name: Place de l'Abbé Georges Hénocque — Crime Location
summary: Crime location in the 13th arrondissement. Near Rue Jenner and the Salpêtrière hospital quarter. Specific case details to be researched.

name: 21 Place des Vosges — Duelling Ground
summary: The Place des Vosges was Paris's foremost duelling ground in the 17th century. Despite Cardinal Richelieu's strict edicts banning duels in 1626 (punishable by death), nobles continued fighting here clandestinely. Dozens died. The gardens under the arcades were a regular scene of blade-work. Victor Hugo, who lived at no. 6 from 1832 to 1848, wrote about the square's violent traditions. Specific crime at no. 21 to be verified.

name: 146 Rue Montmartre — Crime Location
summary: Crime location in the press quarter of the 2nd arrondissement — Rue Montmartre was historically the heart of Parisian journalism. Specific case details to be researched.

name: Rue Racine — Assassination of Symon Petliura
summary: On 25 May 1926, Symon Petliura — former head of the Ukrainian People’s Republic and commander of forces responsible for pogroms that killed tens of thousands of Jews during 1918–21 — was shot five times near a bookshop on Rue Racine by Samuel Schwartzbard, a Jewish watchmaker and anarchist whose family had been killed in the pogroms. Schwartzbard surrendered immediately, declaring he had avenged his people. At trial, his defence lawyer Henri Torres made the case that Petliura bore moral responsibility for the massacres. The jury acquitted Schwartzbard in 1927 after just 35 minutes. The verdict caused an international scandal and became a landmark case in the concept of ‘honour killing’ as political defence.

name: 16 Bd Edgar Quinet — Assassination of Georges Besse
summary: On November 17, 1986, Georges Besse — CEO of Renault and former director of the Eurodif nuclear enrichment consortium — was shot dead on the pavement outside his home by two women from the far-left terrorist group Action Directe, Nathalie Meignen and Nathalie Menigon. He was struck by five bullets at point-blank range. The assassination was coordinated with German group RAF. Action Directe members were convicted and sentenced to life imprisonment in 1989. Besse was the highest-profile victim of French far-left terrorism.

name: Hôtel de Sens — Queen Margot's Execution
summary: In 1605, Marguerite de Valois (Queen Margot), divorced first wife of Henri IV, was granted residence here. In April 1606 she watched from the window as her 18-year-old lover Julien de Chirmont was shot dead on the doorstep by a rival — her own coachman. She refused to cross the threshold until the body was removed, then had the coachman beheaded two days later in the courtyard. One of the last acts of private seigneurial justice in Paris.

name: 9 Rue Aubry le Boucher — Crime Location
summary: Crime location in the 4th arrondissement near the Centre Pompidou area. Specific case details to be researched.

name: McDonald’s — Most Beautiful in Paris
summary: Widely considered the most beautiful McDonald’s in Paris — occupying a grand Haussmann-era commercial space near the Opéra district with high ornamental ceilings, moulded columns and chandeliers surviving incongruously alongside the standard fast-food fit-out. A running gag about the collision of French architectural grandeur and American junk food.

name: Hôtel de Sens — Cannonball in the Wall
summary: One of the oldest surviving medieval residences in Paris, built c.1475–1519 for the Archbishop of Sens. A cannonball has been lodged in the façade since 28 July 1830 — fired during the July Revolution when insurgents clashed with Royal Guard troops in the Marais. It was deliberately left as a memorial. The building also housed Marguerite de Valois (Queen Margot), who reportedly had a young lover executed in its courtyard. Now the Forney library.

name: 29 Bd Haussmann
summary: A notable address in the heart of the grands magasins district in the 9th arrondissement. The boulevard is the epicentre of Haussmann’s commercial vision — anchored by Galeries Lafayette (no. 40) and Printemps (no. 64). Marcel Proust lived at no. 102 from 1906 to 1919, writing much of À la Recherche du Temps Perdu in his cork-lined bedroom.

name: 3 Bd des Capucines — Most Beautiful Starbucks
summary: One of the most spectacular Starbucks locations in the world, housed in a soaring Belle Époque hall with gilded plasterwork ceilings, ornate chandeliers and Corinthian columns on Boulevard des Capucines. The building’s grandeur was designed for a very different tenant — the contrast with the green Starbucks branding is both absurd and magnificent.

name: 51 Rue de Montmorency — Nicolas Flamel’s House
summary: The oldest surviving house in Paris, built in 1407 by Nicolas Flamel — royal scrivener, notary, and the man whose alchemist legend (Philosopher’s Stone) grew long after his actual death in 1418. Flamel inscribed the façade with religious texts and charged tenant scholars only a prayer for the poor in rent. Now a restaurant.

name: 8 Rue Galvani — House with a Tree
summary: A building in the 17th arrondissement where a mature tree has grown through the structure — its trunk rising from a light well inside the building, branches emerging above the roofline and visible from the street. A rare case of urban vegetation overtaking architecture; the tree and building now coexist as a single organism.

name: Rue des Degrés — Shortest Street in Paris
summary: The shortest street in Paris at 5.75 metres, located in the 2nd arrondissement between Rue Beauregard and Rue de Cléry. The “street” consists entirely of a staircase — making it doubly anomalous: the shortest in Paris and the only one that is entirely steps. No buildings front it; it serves purely as a pedestrian shortcut.

name: Le Procope — Oldest Café in Paris
summary: The oldest continuously operating café in Paris, founded in 1686 by Sicilian Francesco Procopio dei Coltelli, who introduced coffee to Parisian society. The intellectual heart of the Enlightenment: Voltaire drank 40 cups of coffee a day here, Rousseau wrote at its tables, Franklin negotiated French support for the American Revolution nearby. During the Revolution, Marat, Robespierre and Danton were regulars. A young Napoleon left his hat as collateral for a bill he couldn’t pay.

name: Stohrer — Oldest Patisserie in Paris
summary: The oldest patisserie in Paris, founded in 1730 by Nicolas Stohrer — pastry chef to Louis XV who followed the royal court from Versailles to Paris. A listed historic monument, still at 51 Rue Montorgueil. Stohrer invented the rum baba. The interior décor was added in 1864 by Paul Baudry, who also painted the Paris Opéra. Specialities: puits d’amour, baba au rhum, religieuse.

name: Rue Eugène Sue — Haussmann Geometry
summary: A street in the 18th arrondissement where Haussmann’s planning becomes visceral. Viewed from above, the facades create an extraordinary rhythmic canyon — identical 6-storey stone apartment blocks with mathematical regularity. A reminder that Haussmann prescribed every cornice height and façade division across a rebuilt city. Best appreciated via satellite view.

name: Café La Roseraie
summary: A café near the Centre Pompidou area. No additional notes recorded — pin saved for future research.

name: Square René Viviani — Oldest Tree in Paris
summary: Home to the oldest tree in Paris — a Robinia pseudoacacia (black locust) planted around 1601 by Jean Robin, botanist to Henri IV, transplanted here in 1636. Now propped up by concrete pillars, it is still alive. The square faces Notre-Dame and gives a direct view of the cathedral’s flying buttresses from the Left Bank.

name: Rue du Chat Qui Pêche — Narrowest Street
summary: At just 1.8 metres wide, the narrowest street in Paris. The name (“Street of the Fishing Cat”) derives from a 15th-century legend about an alchemist’s cat that fished by night in the Seine. Located between Rue de la Huchette and the river in the 5th arrondissement, the street has barely changed since the Middle Ages.

name: Rue Eugène Poubelle — Origin of the Bin
summary: Named after Eugène Poubelle, prefect of the Seine who in 1884 mandated standardized covered waste containers — the first regulated bin system in France. The Figaro mockingly called them "boîtes Poubelle" and the name immediately stuck, entering the dictionary by 1890. Poubelle also introduced early waste separation: bins for organic waste, paper, and broken crockery. The street in the 16th arr. has only one building.

name: 7 Rue Sainte-Anne — Popp Pneumatic Clock
summary: Former site of the central clock of Clément Popp’s Horloges Pneumatiques de Paris — a system distributing precise time signals via compressed air tubes from 1880 to 1927. At peak, over 14,000 public and private clocks across Paris were synchronized through this invisible underground network, receiving a timed air pulse every minute from this building.

name: Rue Denoyez
summary: A short pedestrian alley in Belleville entirely given over to street art since the 1990s. Every wall, shutter, door and downpipe is covered in constantly-changing murals. One of the last surviving legal graffiti zones in Paris — and one of its most visually dense. The walls repaint themselves every few weeks.

name: Café Pli — Temple
summary: Second location of Café Pli — the café where you write a letter to your future self. The café seals and keeps it, mailing it back on a date of your choice. The Temple location is in the northern Marais.

name: Rue Crémieux
summary: One of Paris's most photogenic pedestrian streets, lined with brightly painted houses in pink, blue, yellow and green. Built in 1865, pedestrianized in 1993. Arrive at dawn to shoot without crowds — by 9am it fills with photographers.

name: Mètre-Étalon — Place Vendôme
summary: One of two surviving marble standard metre bars installed across Paris in 1796–97 to teach citizens the new metric unit. Originally 16 were placed around the city. The metre was defined in 1791 as one ten-millionth of the meridian arc from the Equator to the North Pole — the world's first universally defined unit of measurement.

name: Tour de la Liberté — Bastille Remnant
summary: The outline of the Bastille fortress is traced in paving stones on Place de la Bastille. A section of the northeast tower (Tour de la Liberté) was unearthed during Métro Line 5 construction in 1899 and relocated to Square Henri-Galli on Boulevard Henri IV. The exact footprint of each tower is marked in the square.

name: Passage Saint-Maur — Wooden Pavement
summary: One of the last surviving sections of wooden paving (pavé de bois) that once covered many Paris streets in the 19th century. The wooden blocks were laid to muffle the noise of iron-wheeled carriages and horses — a short-lived experiment that mostly rotted away, making this remnant exceptionally rare.

name: 39 Rue du Château d'Eau — Smallest House
summary: The smallest house in Paris, wedged into a gap between two buildings in the 10th arrondissement. It measures just 1.10 metres wide and 5 metres high — barely enough for one narrow room per floor. Originally a sealed passageway between buildings, it was eventually bricked up and converted into a dwelling.

name: Quai de la Mégisserie
summary: The Quai de la Mégisserie has sold live animals, plants and birds from outdoor stalls since the Middle Ages. The name comes from the mégie — the trade of dressing white leather — which dominated the bank before. Today flower merchants still line the quay, continuing a 700-year tradition of riverside commerce beside the Seine.

name: 25 Rue Notre-Dame-de-Nazareth — Dog with Putin's face
summary: Street art on the façade at no. 25 depicting a dog whose face uncannily resembles Vladimir Putin. A classic example of the irreverent political street art that peppers the walls of the 3rd arrondissement.

name: La REcyclerie — Petite Ceinture Bar
summary: An eco-restaurant and urban farm installed in the former Gare Ornano station of the Petite Ceinture — the defunct 32km circular railway that encircled Paris from 1852 until passenger services ceased in 1934. Tables sit on the original platform overlooking the abandoned, now-overgrown tracks. One of the most atmospheric venues in the 18th arrondissement.

name: Impasse des Arbalétriers — Assassination of Louis d'Orléans
summary: On the night of 23 November 1407, Louis I, Duke of Orléans — brother of the mad King Charles VI — was ambushed here by 18 armed men and hacked to death as he rode home from visiting the queen. The murder was ordered by John the Fearless, Duke of Burgundy, and triggered the Armagnac–Burgundian civil war that opened France to English conquest. Joan of Arc's story begins here. A plaque marks the site.

name: Hôtel Lauzun — Club des Hashischins
summary: Built in 1657 on the Île Saint-Louis, this hôtel particulier hosted the Club des Hashischins in the 1840s — a literary society that met monthly to consume dawamesk (a paste of hashish, butter and spices) and document their hallucinations. Members included Théophile Gautier, Eugène Delacroix, and Charles Baudelaire. The experiments directly inspired Baudelaire's Les Paradis Artificiels. Now owned by the City of Paris.

name: Mètre étalon — Rue de Vaugirard
summary: The better-preserved of the two surviving marble standard metre bars, set into the wall at 36 Rue de Vaugirard near the Luxembourg Palace (6th arr.). Installed 1796–97 alongside 15 others across Paris — all but two have since disappeared. The inscription reads simply: "Mètre étalon."

name: Café Pli — Parmentier
summary: Café Pli ("fold", as in a sealed letter) is a unique concept: customers write letters to their future selves, the café stores them sealed, and mails them on a chosen future date — months or years later. A meditative time capsule exercise over a coffee. Two locations in Paris.

name: Belvédère de Belleville
summary: The belvedere at the top of Parc de Belleville offers one of the highest free panoramic views in Paris — Eiffel Tower perfectly framed to the west. At 108m, it sits higher than the Sacré-Cœur terrace. Largely a local secret, not a tourist destination.

name: Casino de Paris
summary: One of Paris's most storied music halls, open since 1890. Hosted Joséphine Baker, Mistinguett, and Maurice Chevalier. Art Deco interior still intact.

name: Roberto
summary: Italian restaurant on Rue de Turenne in the heart of the Marais. The street and surrounding blocks are prime territory for street photography.
